title: Living in Boulder vlog 5 - 10 - 2017: Collecting all Greco-Roman books

enrichment.summary: Kyle gives a brief vlog update about his ongoing project of digitally collecting all surviving Greco-Roman texts. He reflects on the scope of the work—particularly Plato and Aristotle—and shares his goal of calculating how much of human recorded knowledge could theoretically be memorized in a lifetime, comparing brain storage to computer storage.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Enthusiastic and committed; feels he can cover all surviving works including the big ones like Plato and Aristotle
Believes he gains substantial knowledge just from cataloging books—structure, size, scope
Speculates that reading all day every day for a lifetime might fill only about one fifth of the brain's total storage capacity
Interested in estimating how many pages were lost from antiquity based on known gaps in the surviving record

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder Vlog. I didn't get the time to do a video yesterday. I should have.
So this is for May 10th, 2017 at 6.05pm.
So for the last couple days I've been doing books.
And I decided today that I will do everything in the Greco-Roman world.
Everything that we get our history from up until, in the classical world,
Until the fall of Rome, because I was collecting everything from Rome.
And I got, like, I, like, exhausted Rome.
And I looked at Greece, and to get stuff on Greece that I haven't had yet
Is something I could do. It's doable.
The only big ones, things that would take the most time, would be Plato and Aristotle.
All those guys wrote a lot, but it's essential.
So I'm going to collect it.
And I feel like I'm learning so much just by collecting the books,
Just by knowing what the books look like and the structure of them and how big they are.
I can't wait to finish the books and do the word count to know exactly how big they all are.
And get an idea of how big, average-sized, large books are and chapters and all that.
So it's, I'm hooked.
It's been raining, so it's a nice time to stay inside and just work, work, work, work.
Um, I'm working into the morning, and, uh, and then kind of sleeping in a little bit.
But I'm getting a lot done, and I'm listening to books as I'm doing it.
I did, um, which one am I doing?
I was doing Plato Republic just now.
That'll be one of the next books I do.
But, uh, I've just been bouncing around, lots of little, you know, exploring.
And, uh, there's not that much material.
So, you can zoom through it.
So, especially once I get it set up on my website, that's the goal.
To memorize all of them.
Um, find out how many, how many, like, gigabytes.
Or, uh, like, of information the human brain can hold in their lifetime from reading.
Um, just calculate how long it takes to absorb.
And then you can, so, like, kind of like school, put, like, how you'd have different grades.
You would have different ages.
And then you would have different expectations of how much of the knowledge you should have memorized by then.
As your brain is absorbing.
Um, but to find out how many, uh, um, yeah.
Compare storage in the brain to storage in the computer.
But I was thinking, like, you know, if you wanted to, the books would be, if somebody reads all the time, um, all day, every day.
I guess that would be maybe one-fifth of the total storage of your brain.
Um, from all the information, if you were to put it into words.
Um, but, uh, yeah, I was, I wanted, I was thinking about making a map like that.
Um, so, right now I'm working on, um, the Talmud.
Um, and then I'll get going on Plato.
Plato and Aristotle.
That will probably take up the next couple days.
But after I, I conquer those two guys, I'll feel like I'll have the rest of it within grasp.
I'll let them, you know, it's kind of a chore to go and dig around.
Because on Wikisource, there's a book that doesn't exist there about some, some Roman writer talking about something that did exist on the list of Roman writers on Wikipedia.
So, um, but, but, uh, yeah, so I want to make sure to collect all of that.
But I don't think it's much bigger than what you can see on Wikisource.
So it's not that much.
So I'm looking forward to see how it all looks.
Can I condense it all?
Get it all on one page?
Like everything in the Greek or Roman world on one page.
You can see it.
And then you see the number of pages.
And then know exactly how many pages it is.
Um, and then you could do an estimate of how many pages have been lost.
Because you know how many books were lost.
But maybe I'll do that later.
Okay, so that's enough talking for now.
Peace out.
